title: BuildMonitorService.pollOnce(): checkForNewerBuild() is silently swallowed via unregistered mock — newer-build detection path has no positive-case test
description: `pollOnce()` calls `checkForNewerBuild()` (line 223) only on terminal builds. `checkForNewerBuild()` calls `apiClient.getRunningAndQueuedBuilds()`. In `BuildMonitorServiceTest`, `apiClient` is a strict (non-relaxed) mock with no stub for `getRunningAndQueuedBuilds`. The `catch (e: Exception)` in `checkForNewerBuild()` (lines 368-372) swallows the `MockKException` that mockk throws for unstubbed calls, so the tests pass but the newer-build detection is silently skipped in every test that produces a terminal state. No test verifies that `buildState.newerBuild` is populated when a higher-numbered build is running, or that `BuildState.newerBuild` is `null` when no newer builds exist.
suggestedFix: Add `coEvery { apiClient.getRunningAndQueuedBuilds(any()) } returns ApiResult.Success(emptyList())` to `BuildMonitorServiceTest.stubPlanStructure()` (alongside the existing `getPlanStructure` stub). Then add two tests: (1) mock `getRunningAndQueuedBuilds` returning a DTO with `buildNumber = 43` (higher than the current 42) — assert `stateFlow.value?.newerBuild?.buildNumber == 43`; (2) `getRunningAndQueuedBuilds` returns an empty list — assert `stateFlow.value?.newerBuild == null`.
verdictReasoning: Confirmed. BuildMonitorServiceTest.kt line 27 creates a strict (non-relaxed) MockK for apiClient. stubPlanStructure() at line 32 stubs only getPlanStructure; there is no stub for getRunningAndQueuedBuilds anywhere in the file. Every test that exercises a terminal build state (lifeCycleState='Finished' or state='Successful'/'Failed') reaches checkForNewerBuild() at BuildMonitorService.kt line 353, which calls apiClient.getRunningAndQueuedBuilds(). MockK throws MockKException for the unstubbed call. The catch block at lines 368-372 swallows any non-CancellationException and returns null, so tests pass silently but the newer-build detection path is never exercised. No test asserts stateFlow.value?.newerBuild is populated or null. This is a real silent-test-pass situation masking an untested feature path. Adding the stub to stubPlanStructure() is safe and unambiguously correct.
recommendedAction: Add coEvery { apiClient.getRunningAndQueuedBuilds(any()) } returns ApiResult.Success(emptyList()) to BuildMonitorServiceTest.stubPlanStructure() to prevent MockKException swallowing. Then add two tests: (1) getRunningAndQueuedBuilds returns a DTO with buildNumber=43 when current is 42 and assert stateFlow.value?.newerBuild?.buildNumber == 43; (2) returns empty list and assert stateFlow.value?.newerBuild == null.

title: BambooApiClient.downloadArtifact(): no tests at API-client level (only SafetyTest covers basename/containment)
description: `downloadArtifact()` (lines 451-478) has three testable paths: (1) internal URL uses authenticated `httpClient`, external URL uses `sharedPool`; (2) non-2xx response returns `false`; (3) `IOException` returns `false`. `ArtifactDownloadSafetyTest` tests only the `StageDetailPanel.safeArtifactBasename` / `isContainedIn` helpers — it never exercises the actual `BambooApiClient.downloadArtifact()` logic. The cross-origin security boundary (line 457 `isInternal = artifactUrl.startsWith(baseUrl)`) is untested. There is no test that the authenticated client is used for same-origin URLs and the shared pool for cross-origin.
evidence:
```kotlin
val isInternal = artifactUrl.startsWith(baseUrl)
val client = if (isInternal) httpClient else HttpClientFactory.sharedPool
```
— BambooApiClient.kt lines 457-458. No test in `src/test` calls `downloadArtifact()` directly.
suggestedFix: Add a `BambooApiClientDownloadArtifactTest`: (1) use MockWebServer as the base URL, enqueue a 200 with binary body, call `downloadArtifact(server.url("/artifact").toString(), tempFile)`, and assert `true` and file content; (2) enqueue a 404, assert returns `false`; (3) for the cross-origin test, enqueue on a separate second MockWebServer and assert the request has no `Authorization` header (proving `sharedPool` was used, not `httpClient`).
verdictReasoning: Confirmed: ArtifactDownloadSafetyTest (lines 1-120) tests only the StageDetailPanel.safeArtifactBasename and isContainedIn helper methods in the UI layer. No test in any bamboo test file calls BambooApiClient.downloadArtifact() directly. The cross-origin security boundary at lines 457-458 — which intentionally uses sharedPool (no auth headers) for external URLs vs the authenticated httpClient for same-origin — is a security-relevant decision that is entirely untested. This is the most impactful of the coverage findings because the cross-origin token-leakage prevention is a security property. Keeping at Medium severity (not escalating to High/Critical) because no bug is present — the production code is correct — but the untested security boundary is more consequential than style-only coverage gaps.
recommendedAction: Add BambooApiClientDownloadArtifactTest covering: (1) same-origin 200→returns true + file written; (2) non-2xx→returns false; (3) cross-origin request has no Authorization header (proves sharedPool used, not authenticated httpClient).

title: BuildMonitorService.pollOnce(): first-poll BuildFinished suppression behavior has no direct test
description: `pollOnce()` suppresses `BuildFinished` events on the first poll (`isFirstPoll = previousBuildNumber == null`, lines 233-244) to prevent stale "Build Failed" notifications on IDE startup. While `pollOnce emits BuildLogReady on first terminal poll` documents that `BuildLogReady` IS emitted on first poll, there is no test asserting that `BuildFinished` is NOT emitted on the very first terminal poll. The absence of this test means removing the `!isFirstPoll` guard would go undetected by the test suite, causing spurious startup notifications.
suggestedFix: Add a test: configure a terminal build (Successful/Finished) on the first `pollOnce` call and assert via `eventBus.events.test { expectNoEvents() }` that no `BuildFinished` event is emitted. This directly pins the `!isFirstPoll` guard. Pattern: `service.pollOnce("PROJ-BUILD", "main")` then `eventBus.events.test { expectNoEvents() }` (the current test only checks that `BuildLogReady` IS emitted, not that `BuildFinished` is NOT).
verdictReasoning: Confirmed. The existing test 'pollOnce emits BuildLogReady on first terminal poll' (line 189) asserts only that BuildLogReady IS emitted. The comment at line 202 reads 'but NOT BuildFinished' yet the test does not assert absence of BuildFinished. The !isFirstPoll guard at BuildMonitorService.kt line 244 is the sole protection against spurious startup notifications, but removing it would not break any test. This is a real coverage gap for a load-bearing guard. The fix (adding a negative assertion on BuildFinished absence via expectNoEvents or asserting the only event is BuildLogReady) is safe and straightforward.
recommendedAction: Extend the 'pollOnce emits BuildLogReady on first terminal poll' test to assert that no BuildFinished event is emitted on the first poll. Either assert the turbine test block sees exactly one item (BuildLogReady) and then expectNoEvents(), or add a separate test that does a single first-poll on a terminal build and asserts eventBus.events.test { expectNoEvents() } after draining to BuildLogReady.

title: BambooTestResultConverter.fromTestResultsData(): no tests at all — skipped/successful test output paths and mixed-status suites untested
description: `fromTestResultsData()` (lines 94-117) builds a synthetic `BambooTestResultsDto` from the shared domain model and delegates to `toTeamCityMessages()`. It is never called by any test. The existing `BambooTestResultConverterReDoSTest` only calls `toTeamCityMessages()` directly. Additionally, `toTeamCityMessages()` line 78 has a `"skipped"` branch emitting `testIgnored` — no test verifies that a skipped test produces `##teamcity[testIgnored ...]`. Line 33 returns `emptyList()` when `allTests.isEmpty()` — also untested. A test with only passed tests (no failures) is not covered.
evidence:
```kotlin
fun fromTestResultsData(testResults: TestResultsData, buildLog: String? = null): List<String> {
    val failedDtos = testResults.failedTests.map { ft ->
        BambooTestCaseDto(className = ft.className, methodName = ft.methodName, status = "failed")
    }
```
— BambooTestResultConverter.kt lines 94-117. `grep -r 'fromTestResultsData' src/test/` returns zero matches.
suggestedFix: Create `BambooTestResultConverterTest`: (1) call `fromTestResultsData(TestResultsData(total=3, passed=2, failed=1, skipped=1, failedTests=[...]))` and assert the message list contains `testCount count='3'`, `testFailed`, `testIgnored`, and `testFinished`; (2) call with `TestResultsData(total=0, ...)` and assert `emptyList()`; (3) call `toTeamCityMessages` with a `BambooTestResultsDto` where `failedTests` is empty and `successfulTests` has 2 entries — assert only `testFinished` lines are present (no `testFailed`).
verdictReasoning: Confirmed. grep confirms fromTestResultsData() is called only from BuildDashboardPanel.kt line 1210 in production and has zero test coverage. BambooTestResultConverterReDoSTest calls toTeamCityMessages() directly. Three untested paths are real: (1) the 'skipped' branch at BambooTestResultConverter.kt line 76 that emits testIgnored; (2) the allTests.isEmpty() early return at line 34 when called via fromTestResultsData with zero total tests; (3) a passing-only result set (no testFailed lines). The method constructs a synthetic BambooTestResultsDto with successfulTests always empty (line 113), meaning passed tests are silently dropped — this is a real behavioral gap beyond just coverage. The suggested fix is safe and does not risk removing any load-bearing fallback.
recommendedAction: Create BambooTestResultConverterTest covering: (1) fromTestResultsData with a mixed result containing skipped tests — assert testIgnored lines present; (2) fromTestResultsData with TestResultsData(total=0, ...) — assert emptyList(); (3) toTeamCityMessages with a dto where failedTests is empty and successfulTests has entries — assert only testFinished lines, no testFailed. Also verify that the successfulTests=empty construction in fromTestResultsData (line 113) is intentional, since it discards passed test names.

title: BambooApiClient.get(): no test for 429 rate-limit or 5xx server-error HTTP responses
description: The `get()` private method maps HTTP 429 to `RATE_LIMITED` and any other non-2xx to `SERVER_ERROR` (lines 413-414). No test in any ApiClient test file exercises 429 or 500/503 responses against any of the public endpoints (`getLatestResult`, `getPlans`, `getBranches`, etc.). The `BambooApiClientTest` only tests 401, the XSRF path, and specific 404s, leaving the two most common transient-failure codes completely untested.
suggestedFix: Add to `BambooApiClientTest`: (1) `server.enqueue(MockResponse().setResponseCode(429))` then call `getPlans()` and assert `(result as ApiResult.Error).type == ErrorType.RATE_LIMITED`; (2) `server.enqueue(MockResponse().setResponseCode(503))` and assert `ErrorType.SERVER_ERROR`. Additionally test that `getLatestResult` on a 500 returns `SERVER_ERROR`, since that method is the polling hot path. Use `@ParameterizedTest` with `@ValueSource(ints = [429, 500, 502, 503])` to keep it concise.
verdictReasoning: Confirmed real: searching all test files under `bamboo/src/test/` finds zero tests exercising `429` or any 5xx response code. `BambooApiClientTest` covers 401, XSRF, 403, 404, and specific formats but not 429 or 5xx. `BambooApiClient.get()` at lines 413–414 maps 429 to `RATE_LIMITED` and all other non-2xx codes to `SERVER_ERROR` — these paths exist in production but have zero test coverage. However, severity is downgraded from High to Low: (1) the mapping logic (a `when` branch on the HTTP code) is trivially correct by inspection and not subject to regression; (2) 429 and 5xx are handled identically to 403/404 in the surrounding `when` block so there is no special logic to verify; (3) the callers all propagate `ApiResult.Error` up correctly. The missing tests represent a coverage gap but not a likely source of bugs. Fix is not harmful.
recommendedAction: Add tests to `BambooApiClientTest`: one for 429 asserting `ErrorType.RATE_LIMITED` and at least one for a 5xx (e.g. 503) asserting `ErrorType.SERVER_ERROR`. A `@ParameterizedTest` with `@ValueSource(ints = [500, 502, 503])` keeps it concise.

title: BambooApiClient.get(): no test for IOException/network-failure propagation to NETWORK_ERROR
description: The `get()` catch block (line 417-419) wraps `IOException` as `ApiResult.Error(ErrorType.NETWORK_ERROR)`. No existing MockWebServer-based test exercises this path — all tests enqueue valid responses. `BuildMonitorServiceTest.pollOnce handles API error gracefully` mocks the result at the mock layer, not at the HTTP layer, so the IOException branch is never triggered in any test. This path is the most common production failure (firewall drops, VPN disconnects, DNS failures).
suggestedFix: In `BambooApiClientTest`, call `server.shutdown()` before making the request (MockWebServer returns a connection-refused IOException) and assert `(result as ApiResult.Error).type == ErrorType.NETWORK_ERROR`. Test this for at least `getLatestResult`, `getPlans`, and `queueBuildWithStageSelection` as they are the highest-traffic endpoints. Pattern: `server.shutdown(); val result = client.getLatestResult("PROJ-BUILD"); assertTrue(result is ApiResult.Error); assertEquals(ErrorType.NETWORK_ERROR, (result as ApiResult.Error).type)`.
verdictReasoning: The IOException catch block at BambooApiClient.kt lines 417-419 wraps network failures as ApiResult.Error(ErrorType.NETWORK_ERROR), which is correct production code. The gap is confirmed: all server.shutdown() calls in the test files appear only in @AfterEach teardown methods (not before a live request), so no test causes a real IOException at the HTTP layer. The production behavior is already correct — this is a pure test-coverage gap, not a behavioral bug. The suggested fix (call server.shutdown() before the request to trigger connection-refused) is safe and documents the existing contract. Severity downgraded from High to Low because the production code path is correct and already tested indirectly via mock-layer tests; only the HTTP-layer exercise is absent.
recommendedAction: Add a test in BambooApiClientTest that calls server.shutdown() before making a request and asserts ErrorType.NETWORK_ERROR. Covers at least getLatestResult and getPlans as the highest-traffic endpoints.

title: BambooApiClient.get(): no test for non-JSON 200 response body triggering PARSE_ERROR
description: Lines 393-399 check for an unexpected `Content-Type` on a 2xx response and return `PARSE_ERROR` (e.g., when a reverse proxy returns `text/html` for a valid endpoint). Lines 402-407 catch JSON parse failures and return `PARSE_ERROR`. Neither branch is tested. The `AUTH_REDIRECT` path (HTML body on `rerunFailedJobs`/`enablePlanBranch`) is covered via `postForm`, but the equivalent GET-path protection (non-JSON Content-Type guard on `get()`) has no test.
suggestedFix: Add to `BambooApiClientTest`: (1) `server.enqueue(MockResponse().setResponseCode(200).setHeader("Content-Type", "text/html").setBody("<html>proxy page</html>"))`; call `getPlans()` and assert `ErrorType.PARSE_ERROR`. (2) `server.enqueue(MockResponse().setBody("not-valid-json"))` then call `getLatestResult("PROJ-BUILD")` and assert `ErrorType.PARSE_ERROR`.
verdictReasoning: Both the content-type guard (lines 393-399) and the JSON parse-failure catch (lines 402-407) inside the get() method exist and are correct. No test in any BambooApiClient* test file exercises either path — all tests enqueue valid JSON responses. The AUTH_REDIRECT path in postForm is covered by rerunFailedJobs and enablePlanBranch tests, but the analogous GET-path content-type guard is not. This is a pure coverage gap; the production code is correct. Severity downgraded from Medium to Low since this is not a behavioral bug.
recommendedAction: Add two tests to BambooApiClientTest: (1) enqueue a 200 with Content-Type: text/html and assert ErrorType.PARSE_ERROR; (2) enqueue a 200 with malformed JSON body and assert ErrorType.PARSE_ERROR.

title: BambooApiClient.paginate(): no test for error returned on a non-first page
description: The `paginate()` helper (lines 624-651) short-circuits on any page error via `is ApiResult.Error -> return result`. The existing pagination tests only cover the success path (single-page, two-page, echoed-start-index quirk) and a single first-page 401 failure. No test exercises the case where the first page succeeds but a subsequent page returns an error (e.g., the server goes down or returns 500 mid-pagination). This means the aggregated partial result is silently discarded and the caller sees a network error rather than partial data.
suggestedFix: Add to `BambooApiClientPaginationTest`: enqueue a full page-1 success (100 items) then enqueue a 500 for page 2. Call `getPlans()` and assert the result is `ApiResult.Error` (not partial success). This documents the current contract that a mid-pagination error discards all collected data, making the behavior explicit and catchable.
verdictReasoning: The paginate() helper at lines 634-636 returns the error immediately when any page fails, discarding all previously collected items. BambooApiClientPaginationTest covers: single-page, two-page, first-page error, and the server-echoes-start-index-0 quirk. There is no test for the case where page 1 succeeds but page 2+ returns an error. The current behavior (discard partial results on mid-pagination error) is a documented design choice, not a bug. The finding correctly identifies the gap. Severity downgraded from Medium to Low: the partial-discard semantics are intentional and the fix only adds a documenting test.
recommendedAction: Add a test that enqueues a full first page followed by a 500, calls getPlans(), and asserts ApiResult.Error to document the contract that mid-pagination errors discard all collected data.

title: BambooApiClient.stopBuild(): no error-path tests (only verifies the X-Atlassian-Token header on 200)
description: The only test for `stopBuild` (`stopBuild sets X-Atlassian-Token header`, `BambooApiClientTest` line 360) verifies the HTTP method and header on a 200 response. There is no test for 401 (`AUTH_FAILED`), 403 (`FORBIDDEN`), 404 (`NOT_FOUND`), the `AUTH_REDIRECT` path (200+HTML), or `NETWORK_ERROR` (IOException). The `put()` method handles all these cases (lines 597-605) but they are untested. The `BambooServiceImpl.stopBuild()` maps each error type to a specific hint string that is equally untested.
evidence:
```kotlin
@Test
fun `stopBuild sets X-Atlassian-Token header`() = runTest {
    server.enqueue(MockResponse().setResponseCode(200))
    client.stopBuild("PROJ-BUILD-42")
    val recorded = server.takeRequest()
    assertEquals("PUT", recorded.method)
    assertEquals("no-check", recorded.getHeader("X-Atlassian-Token"))
}
```
— BambooApiClientTest.kt lines 360-368. Only one test for `stopBuild`, only the success/header path.
suggestedFix: Add to `BambooApiClientTest`: (1) `server.enqueue(MockResponse().setResponseCode(401))` → assert `ErrorType.AUTH_FAILED`; (2) 403 → `FORBIDDEN`; (3) 404 → `NOT_FOUND`; (4) 200 with `Content-Type: text/html` → `AUTH_REDIRECT` (mirrors the existing `cancelBuild` test pattern at line 337 and `rerunFailedJobs` at line 387).
verdictReasoning: Confirmed: there is exactly one test for stopBuild (lines 360-368 in BambooApiClientTest.kt), covering only the happy-path header check on a 200 response. The put() method at lines 574-606 handles 401→AUTH_FAILED, 403→FORBIDDEN, 404→NOT_FOUND, and AUTH_REDIRECT on 200+HTML. None of these error paths are tested for stopBuild. All other write operations (cancelBuild, rerunFailedJobs, enablePlanBranch) have error-path tests. The production code is correct; this is a test-coverage gap. Severity downgraded from Medium to Low.
recommendedAction: Add error-path tests for stopBuild: 401→AUTH_FAILED, 403→FORBIDDEN, 404→NOT_FOUND, and 200 with text/html content-type→AUTH_REDIRECT, mirroring the existing cancelBuild and enablePlanBranch test patterns.

title: BambooApiClient.queueBuildWithStageSelection(): no test for parse failure on the queue-response body
description: Lines 269-275 catch a JSON parse failure on the `BambooQueueResponse` body and return `ErrorType.PARSE_ERROR`. All existing tests for `queueBuildWithStageSelection` supply well-formed JSON responses. No test exercises a successful (200) POST to the queue endpoint followed by a malformed JSON body (e.g., `{"buildResultKey":null}` missing required fields or truncated JSON). This is a real edge case when Bamboo returns a redirect or HTML page with status 200.
suggestedFix: Add to `BambooApiClientStageSelectionTest`: `server.enqueue(MockResponse().setResponseCode(200).setBody("not-json"))`; call `queueBuildWithStageSelection("PROJ-BUILD", emptyMap(), null)` and assert `(result as ApiResult.Error).type == ErrorType.PARSE_ERROR`.
verdictReasoning: Lines 269-275 catch a JSON deserialization exception when parsing the BambooQueueResponse body and return ErrorType.PARSE_ERROR. All tests in BambooApiClientStageSelectionTest supply well-formed JSON in their responses. No test enqueues a malformed body for a 200 response on the queue endpoint. The production catch clause is correct. This is a pure coverage gap. Severity downgraded from Medium to Low.
recommendedAction: Add a test in BambooApiClientStageSelectionTest that enqueues a 200 response with body 'not-json' and asserts ErrorType.PARSE_ERROR.

title: BambooServiceImpl.getTestResults(): no tests at service level for success, error, or not-configured paths
description: `BambooServiceImpl.getTestResults()` (lines 200-239) maps the `BambooJobTestResultDto` into a `TestResultsData` with `FailedTestData` entries, covering three branches: (a) not configured, (b) API error, (c) success with failed/successful test counts. None of these branches has a test. The only existing `BambooTestResultConverter` tests (`BambooTestResultConverterReDoSTest`) target the static converter directly and do not go through the service layer. There is no test that skipped-test counts are correctly propagated, that an empty failed-tests list produces `failedTests = emptyList()`, or that an API error from `getTestResults()` surfaces as `isError = true`.
evidence:
```kotlin
override suspend fun getTestResults(resultKey: String): ToolResult<TestResultsData> {
    val api = client ?: return ToolResult(
        data = TestResultsData(total = 0, passed = 0, failed = 0, skipped = 0),
        ...
    )
```
— BambooServiceImpl.kt lines 200-240. `grep -r 'getTestResults' src/test/` returns zero matches.
suggestedFix: Create `BambooServiceImplTestResultsTest` with three cases: (1) mock `getTestResults` returning a DTO with 5 passed, 2 failed, 1 skipped — assert `data.total==8`, `data.failed==2`; (2) mock returning `ApiResult.Error(ErrorType.NOT_FOUND, "...")` — assert `isError==true`; (3) set `testClientOverride = null` (or do not set it, leaving `client` null) and assert `isError==true` with the not-configured hint.
verdictReasoning: Confirmed via grep: zero matches for 'getTestResults' in the entire bamboo/src/test/ tree. BambooServiceImpl.getTestResults() (lines 200-239) has three distinct branches: not-configured (client null), API error, and success with mapping. BambooTestResultConverterReDoSTest covers the static converter directly, not the service layer. The production code is correct. This is a pure service-layer coverage gap. Severity downgraded from Medium to Low.
recommendedAction: Create BambooServiceImplTestResultsTest with three cases: null client→isError=true with not-configured hint; API error→isError=true; success DTO→correct data mapping (total, passed, failed, skipped counts and FailedTestData list).

title: BambooServiceImpl.getPlanVariables(): no test for all-strategies-fail path returning isError=true
description: `getPlanVariables()` (lines 307-372) has a three-strategy fallback: Strategy A (`variableContext`), then Strategy C (most-recent build's `getBuildVariables`). The existing `BambooServiceImplStrategyCTest` only tests the A-fails→C-succeeds branch. The all-strategies-fail path (lines 361-372) — when variableContext returns empty and Strategy C also fails — returns `isError = true`. This path has zero test coverage. Additionally, the Strategy A returning empty list (line 319 `contextResult.data.isNotEmpty()`) before falling to Strategy C is untested for the case where variableContext succeeds but with zero items.
suggestedFix: Add to `BambooServiceImplStrategyCTest` or a new file: (1) mock `getPlanVariableContext` returning `ApiResult.Error` and `getRecentResults` also returning `ApiResult.Error` — assert `result.isError == true`; (2) mock `getPlanVariableContext` returning `ApiResult.Success(emptyList())` and `getRecentResults` returning an empty build list — assert `result.isError == true` (no builds to fall back to).
verdictReasoning: BambooServiceImplStrategyCTest has exactly one test: variableContext error→Strategy C succeeds. The all-strategies-fail path (lines 361-371 in BambooServiceImpl.kt) — where both variableContext and Strategy C fail — is not tested. Additionally, the case where variableContext returns ApiResult.Success(emptyList()) (triggering Strategy C) and then Strategy C also fails is untested. Both paths return isError=true. The production code is correct. This is a coverage gap on the error return at lines 366-371. Severity downgraded from Medium to Low.
recommendedAction: Add tests: (1) variableContext returns ApiResult.Error AND getRecentResults returns ApiResult.Error → result.isError==true; (2) variableContext returns ApiResult.Success(emptyList()) AND getRecentResults returns empty list (no builds) → result.isError==true with the 'variableContext returned empty' message.

title: BuildLogParser.parse(): no test for empty/blank input string
description: The `parse()` method (line 26) calls `buildLog.lineSequence()` which works on any string including empty. The tests cover: log with errors, log with warnings, clean log, and generic error. No test exercises `parse("")` or `parse("   \n   ")` (blank/whitespace input). The `.count { it == '\n' }` call on line 28 on an empty string is harmless but untested. Since `getBuildLog()` returns the raw string from the server (and documents that empty body returns `""`), a blank log passed through to the parser should produce an empty list without any indexing failure.
evidence:
```kotlin
fun parse(buildLog: String): List<BuildError> {
    val lines = buildLog.lineSequence()
    log.info("[Bamboo:Parser] Starting build log parsing (~${buildLog.count { it == '\n' } + 1} lines)")
```
— BuildLogParser.kt lines 26-28. `BuildLogParserTest` has 4 tests, none with blank/empty input.
suggestedFix: Add to `BuildLogParserTest`: (1) `BuildLogParser.parse("")` → assert `isEmpty()`; (2) `BuildLogParser.parse("  \n  \n  ")` → assert `isEmpty()`. These are trivial to add and pin the boundary behavior.
verdictReasoning: Confirmed as a coverage gap, but with reassessed severity. The 'returns empty list for clean log' test at BuildLogParserTest.kt line 41 uses a non-empty string that produces no matches, which exercises most of the same code path as empty input. The claim that parse("") and parse("  \n  ") are untested is strictly true. The code at line 27 calls buildLog.count { it == '\n' } + 1 on an empty string which returns 1 (harmless), and lineSequence() on empty input yields no lines, returning emptyList(). There is no crash risk. The gap is real but the actual behavioral coverage difference is minimal.
recommendedAction: Add two trivial tests to BuildLogParserTest: parse("") -> assertEmpty() and parse("  \n  \n  ") -> assertEmpty(). These are safe to add and pin the boundary contract.

title: BambooServiceImpl.client lazy property: URL-change invalidation not tested — stale cached client risk
description: The `client` property (lines 56-72) caches a `BambooApiClient` keyed by `cachedBaseUrl`. If `settings.connections.bambooUrl` changes mid-session (e.g., the user updates the settings page), `url != cachedBaseUrl` triggers a rebuild. No test exercises this state transition: all service tests inject `testClientOverride` and bypass the caching logic entirely. A bug in the invalidation path (e.g., a threading race on `@Volatile` without synchronization when the URL and client are written non-atomically) would go undetected.
evidence:
```kotlin
if (url != cachedBaseUrl || cachedClient == null) {
    cachedBaseUrl = url
    ...
    cachedClient = BambooApiClient(...)
}
return cachedClient
```
— BambooServiceImpl.kt lines 61-70. No test covers the URL-change branch; all tests set `testClientOverride`.
suggestedFix: Add a test (without `testClientOverride`) that sets `settings.connections.bambooUrl` to URL-A, calls `service.client`, then changes to URL-B, calls `service.client` again, and asserts the returned client targets URL-B (not URL-A). Since this requires controlling `PluginSettings`, use a mock `Project` that returns a controllable settings instance — already the pattern used in `BambooServiceImplStrategyCTest`. Assert that two different `BambooApiClient` instances are returned, not the same cached one.
verdictReasoning: Confirmed as a coverage gap. All tests that exercise BambooServiceImpl use testClientOverride, bypassing the client property entirely (BambooServiceImpl.kt lines 57-72). The URL-change invalidation branch (line 61: url != cachedBaseUrl) is never reached by any test. The @Volatile on cachedBaseUrl and cachedClient provides visibility guarantees but not atomicity — a thread reading between the cachedBaseUrl = url write (line 62) and the cachedClient = BambooApiClient(...) write (line 66) sees a stale client for one URL under a new URL. This is a real but low-probability race on the cache miss path. Severity is reassessed to Low because: (1) URL changes are rare user-driven events, not concurrent API calls; (2) a stale client for one missed call simply fails and retries on next poll. Adding the test requires a controllable PluginSettings, which is complex.
recommendedAction: Add a test that creates BambooServiceImpl without testClientOverride, uses a mock Project returning a controllable PluginSettings instance, calls service.client with URL-A, changes the bambooUrl to URL-B, calls service.client again, and asserts the second call returns a different BambooApiClient instance targeting URL-B. Alternatively, document the non-atomic write as accepted risk.

title: BuildMonitorService.switchBranch(): stateFlow reset to null before re-poll not tested
description: `switchBranch()` (lines 203-207) resets `_stateFlow.value = null` then calls `startPolling()`. This reset is important for the UI — the panel must clear the displayed build state before showing the new branch's build. No test verifies that `stateFlow.value` is null immediately after `switchBranch()` is called and before the poller fires the first result. `BuildMonitorFocusLifecycleTest` covers the polling lifecycle via the focus flow (which calls `startPolling`) but does NOT test the `switchBranch` path or the intermediate null state.
evidence:
```kotlin
fun switchBranch(planKey: String, newBranch: String, intervalMs: Long = 30_000) {
    log.info("[Bamboo:Monitor] Switching branch to '$newBranch' for planKey=$planKey")
    _stateFlow.value = null
    startPolling(planKey, newBranch, intervalMs)
}
```
— BuildMonitorService.kt lines 203-207. No test in `BuildMonitorServiceTest` or `BuildMonitorFocusLifecycleTest` calls `switchBranch()`.
suggestedFix: Add to `BuildMonitorServiceTest`: call `pollOnce` to populate `stateFlow` with a non-null value, then call `switchBranch("PROJ-BUILD", "other-branch")`, and assert `service.stateFlow.value == null` immediately after (before the next poll). Use turbine to snapshot the flow: `stateFlow.test { ... awaitItem() shouldBe null ... }`.
verdictReasoning: Confirmed. No test in BuildMonitorServiceTest or BuildMonitorFocusLifecycleTest calls switchBranch(). BuildMonitorService.kt lines 203-207 resets _stateFlow.value = null then calls startPolling. The intermediate null state is load-bearing for the UI (panel must clear before showing new branch data) but is completely untested. BuildMonitorFocusLifecycleTest scenario 5 tests that stateFlow is null when startPolling is intercepted, but this does not cover the switchBranch path which explicitly sets null then starts polling. The fix is safe to add.
recommendedAction: Add a test to BuildMonitorServiceTest: call pollOnce to populate stateFlow, then call service.switchBranch('PROJ-BUILD', 'other-branch'), assert service.stateFlow.value == null immediately after (before the next poll completes). Use turbine to snapshot the intermediate null emission.

title: PlanDetectionService.validate(): concurrent-call race on Mutex not tested — two concurrent validators for same key both make API calls
description: `validate()` (lines 128-156) releases the `cacheMutex` around the HTTP call (intentionally, to avoid blocking) and re-acquires it to write the result. The comment says "Two concurrent validators racing on the same candidate may both call the API once, but the writes are idempotent". No test exercises concurrent validation of the same key. The `in-memory positive cache avoids repeated API calls within a session` test (line 550 in `PlanDetectionServiceTest`) calls `validate()` serially, not concurrently. A concurrent call could expose a bug if the mutex is incorrectly held across the network call.
suggestedFix: Add a test that launches two coroutines simultaneously both calling `service.validate("PROJ-MAIN")` with the API client mock configured with a small delay. Use `coVerify(atMost = 2) { apiClient.validatePlan("PROJ-MAIN") }` to assert the API is called at most twice (not more, which would indicate the cache read is broken). Use `runTest` with two `launch { validate(...) }` blocks and `joinAll()`.
verdictReasoning: Confirmed as a coverage gap. PlanDetectionServiceTest line 550 calls validate() serially twice and verifies coVerify(exactly = 1) that the API was called once (positive cache hit). No test exercises two concurrent coroutines both calling validate() for the same key simultaneously. The validate() implementation at PlanDetectionService.kt lines 128-156 intentionally drops cacheMutex around the HTTP call (lines 137-144) and re-acquires it only for the write (lines 147-155), as documented in the KDoc at lines 119-126. The design is correct: both concurrent callers make one API call each (at most 2 total), and writes are idempotent. The risk of an actual concurrency bug in the current implementation is very low given the documented intent and the two-phase lock design. This is a coverage gap for a documented concurrent design, not evidence of a bug.
recommendedAction: Add a test using runTest with two parallel launch blocks both calling service.validate('PROJ-MAIN') with a coEvery stub that introduces a small delay. After joinAll(), verify coVerify(atMost = 2) { apiClient.validatePlan('PROJ-MAIN') } and that both calls returned true. This pins the documented behavior without risking breakage.
